events.event_label: LOC_Os10g31660.1, LOC_Os10g31680.1, LOC_Os10g31720.1 encode glycine-rich cell wall structural proteins
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p055.s001: Compared to the mapping of quantitative trait loci (QTLs) for drought tolerance on rice chromosomes (QTL data from Q-TARO database, http://qtaro.abr.affrc.go.jp/), we observed a significant association between the hub gene clusters and QTLs for drought tolerance, and a total of 18 hub gene clusters overlapped with these QTLs, which was far beyond expected by chance (p<0.005) (Figure 8). PMC3360736.p055.s002: For instance, the hub gene cluster of chr10.1 overlapped with two QTLs that were associated with the root penetration index and the drought response index. PMC3360736.p055.s003: It is interesting that this hub gene cluster contains nine drought-responsive genes and three of them (LOC_Os10g31660.1, LOC_Os10g31680.1 and LOC_Os10g31720.1) encode glycine-rich cell wall structural proteins in module 1. PMC3360736.p055.s004: It is possible that the reduction of glycine-rich proteins is associated with the remodeling of vessel cell walls in rice roots during drought stress.

events.event_label: OsbZip23, OsbZip72 and AP37 affect drought tolerance traits
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p058.s003: These correlated transcriptional modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and TF binding sites. PMC3360736.p058.s004: Functional annotations show that some modules are enriched for genes involved in the response to stress, including OsbZip23, OsbZip72 and AP37 which have been validated as important factors affecting drought tolerance traits for rice. PMC3360736.p058.s005: Although approximately 20% of module genes still have not been functionally annotated, the high degree of transcriptional connectivity allows us to infer the role of these novel genes in drought response based on the known annotations of the stress-related genes in the modules.

events.event_label: drought stress alters expression of thousands of rice genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p058.s001: The major objective for this study was to use the network-based approach to identify drought-responsive gene modules, providing new insights into the organization of functional modules in the response to drought stress. PMC3360736.p058.s002: From publically available microarray data, we identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress and more than half of the drought-responsive genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 functional modules. PMC3360736.p058.s003: These correlated transcriptional modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and TF binding sites. PMC3360736.p058.s004: Functional annotations show that some modules are enriched for genes involved in the response to stress, including OsbZip23, OsbZip72 and AP37 which have been validated as important factors affecting drought tolerance traits for rice.

events.event_label: bZIP and heat shock transcription factor bind to ABRE element
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p059.s003: Motif enrichment analysis revealed approximately 70% genes in the module contained the ABRE element in their promoter regions, and most of them are induced by drought stress. PMC3360736.p059.s004: The enrichment of ABRE element in module 4 suggests that the module may be involved in ABA-mediated drought response pathway. PMC3360736.p059.s005: The ABRE element has been shown to be recognized by the bZIP belonging to the AREB/ABFs family, but interestingly, but interestingly, we found a novel candidate gene in module 4 that encodes a heat shock transcription factor which binds to ABRE elements. PMC3360736.p059.s006: The HSF TF contains 35 putative target genes with at least one ABRE element in their promoter regions, implying that it is likely to serve as a key regulator of the module involved in drought response.

events.event_label: Module 8 is upregulated by drought stress and contributes to abiotic stimulus response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p030.s001: Module 8 is another module that is upregulated by drought stress, which is principally involved in the response to abiotic stimuli. PMC3360736.p030.s002: The gene module contains 14 abiotic stimulus responsive genes (GO: 0009628, FDR = 1.9×10−5), representing approximately 38% of all of the 37 annotated genes in the module. PMC3360736.p030.s003: These data suggest that modules 4 and 8 may serve as key positive players in rice response to drought stress.

events.event_label: hub genes are clustered on rice chromosomes and belong to drought-responsive modules
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p060.s001: Gene organization in rice chromosomes is non-random with respect to the complex traits of drought tolerance. PMC3360736.p060.s002: The distribution of drought-responsive hub genes that was detected varied both among the rice chromosomes and within regions of each chromosome. PMC3360736.p060.s003: Many hub genes in the drought-responsive modules were clustered in rice chromosomes. PMC3360736.p060.s004: These hub gene clusters overlapped with QTLs for drought tolerance, which suggests that hub gene clusters are likely to be major components of drought tolerance QTLs.

events.event_label: drought stress alters expression of thousands of genes and forms coexpression modules
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p002.s001: Understanding the molecular mechanisms that underlie plant responses to drought stress is challenging due to the complex interplay of numerous different genes. PMC3360736.p002.s002: Here, we used network-based gene clustering to uncover the relationships between drought-responsive genes from large microarray datasets. PMC3360736.p002.s003: We identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress; 1,392 genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 gene modules. PMC3360736.p002.s004: These drought-responsive gene modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and transcription factor binding sites.

events.event_label: Module 4 enriched for embryonic development genes including LEA genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p029.s001: Module 4 is enriched for genes known to control the response of plants to water (GO: 0009415, FDR = 3.7×10−8). PMC3360736.p029.s002: The module contains five genes involved in this process, which accounted for 41.6% of the annotated water responsive genes in the background datasets. PMC3360736.p029.s003: Functional enrichment revealed that the module was also enriched for genes involved in embryonic development (GO: 0009790, FDR = 4.7×10−6), including six annotated late embryogenesis abundant LEA genes. PMC3360736.p029.s004: It is of interest that almost all genes within the module are significantly induced by drought stress.

events.event_label: Module 8 upregulated by drought stress and contributes to abiotic stimulus response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p030.s001: Module 8 is another module that is upregulated by drought stress, which is principally involved in the response to abiotic stimuli. PMC3360736.p030.s002: The gene module contains 14 abiotic stimulus responsive genes (GO: 0009628, FDR = 1.9×10−5), representing approximately 38% of all of the 37 annotated genes in the module. PMC3360736.p030.s003: These data suggest that modules 4 and 8 may serve as key positive players in rice response to drought stress.

events.event_label: Module 1 highly expressed in roots and part of drought-responsive network
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p033.s001: Expression patterns of drought-responsive module genes were investigated in six different tissue types, including the anther, embryo, root, leaf, shoot and shoot apical meristem (SAM), which are shown in Figure 5. PMC3360736.p033.s002: As the largest gene group in the drought-responsive network, module 1 is enriched for stimulus response genes with high expression levels in roots. PMC3360736.p033.s003: Transcripts of module 2 are abundant in leaves and shoots, which is consistent with the fact that the module genes are preferentially involved in photosynthesis. PMC3360736.p033.s004: Not surprisingly, module 4, which is enriched for LEA genes, is highly expressed in the embryo.

events.event_label: ABRE and GCC-box motifs participate in drought stress response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p037.s003: A total of 51 putative cis-motifs were found in 15 drought-responsive modules with MEME E-value less than 1. PMC3360736.p037.s004: The Pearson correlation coefficient between each motif pair was calculated based on the corresponding position weight matrices and similar motifs were clustered using a correlation coefficient cutoff of 0.65. PMC3360736.p037.s005: After merging motifs within each cluster, 10 nonredundant motifs were identified to be involved in the response to drought stress (Figure 6). PMC3360736.p037.s006: A comparison of the discovered motifs with the cis-regulatory elements from the PLACE and AGRIS databases showed that some motifs, such as ABA-responsive element (ABRE) and GCC-box, were known to be involved in drought stress response. PMC3360736.p037.s007: As expected, the ABRE element with the conserved GCCACGTGKC sequence was found to be enriched in six modules, including 4, 8, and 14, which were significantly induced by drought stress.

events.event_label: Drought stress induces HSF expression which shows high intramodular connectivity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p045.s001: Module 4 contains four genes encoding transcription factors from three families. PMC3360736.p045.s002: Interestingly, a heat shock transcription factor (HSF, LOC_Os01g39020.1) induced by drought stress had the highest intramodular connectivity value in the drought-responsive TFs. PMC3360736.p045.s003: The HSF gene contained 35 co-expression neighbors and their average correlation to the locus was 0.90 (Figure 7A). PMC3360736.p045.s004: Many HSF co-expression genes encode stress-related functional proteins, such as the LEA and dehydrin proteins.

events.event_label: ABRE and GCC-box motifs are conserved cis-regulatory elements in target genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p045.s003: The HSF gene contained 35 co-expression neighbors and their average correlation to the locus was 0.90 (Figure 7A). PMC3360736.p045.s004: Many HSF co-expression genes encode stress-related functional proteins, such as the LEA and dehydrin proteins. PMC3360736.p045.s005: Prediction of cis-regulatory elements revealed that the ABRE element and the GCC-box were conserved in these putative target genes; in particular, the ABRE element was found in all 35 neighboring genes. PMC3360736.p045.s006: To test the reliability of the inferred HSF regulatory network in the response to drought stress, the HSF and its five neighboring genes were further investigated for their stress response by quantitative RT-PCR analysis in the upland rice cultivar, IRAT109. PMC3360736.p045.s007: Expression analysis showed that these genes, including HSF, were induced by drought stress (simulated by polyethylene glycol, PEG) and abscisic acid (ABA); furthermore, their expression patterns exhibited obvious coherence in IRAT109 seedlings under PEG and ABA treatments (Figure 7B).

events.event_label: Hub genes participate in stimulus response and are associated with drought stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p049.s001: The highly connected hub genes within the networks are thought to play important roles in organizing the functional modules. PMC3360736.p049.s002: Here, we identified hub genes as the top 20% highly connected nodes in each module, and 262 hub genes were found in fifteen drought-responsive modules. PMC3360736.p049.s003: Functional enrichment revealed that these drought-responsive hub genes were preferentially involved in the response to stimulus (Table 2), which was in accordance with the important roles of these genes in response to drought stress.

events.event_label: Module 2 expressed in leaf and shoot and involved in photosynthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p033.s001: Expression patterns of drought-responsive module genes were investigated in six different tissue types, including the anther, embryo, root, leaf, shoot and shoot apical meristem (SAM), which are shown in Figure 5. PMC3360736.p033.s003: Transcripts of module 2 are abundant in leaves and shoots, which is consistent with the fact that the module genes are preferentially involved in photosynthesis. events.event_label: Motifs REGION1OSOSEM, SBOXATRBCS, and GAGA8HVBKN3 associate with multiple drought-responsive modules
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p037.s006: A comparison of the discovered motifs with the cis-regulatory elements from the PLACE and AGRIS databases showed that some motifs, such as ABA-responsive element (ABRE) and GCC-box, were known to be involved in drought stress response. PMC3360736.p037.s008: Among those revealed elements, most motifs were described to be involved in known functions, while only one motif with the AGCTAGCTAG sequence had an unknown function. PMC3360736.p037.s009: We found many of discovered motifs were enriched in more than one drought-responsive gene module, especially the three global motifs that included the REGION1OSOSEM (CSGCGGCGGC), the SBOXATRBCS (CCCCCTCC) and the GAGA8HVBKN3 (GGGAGGGAG) element which occurred in nine or more modules. events.event_label: drought-responsive modules enriched in stimulus response, photosynthesis, nucleosome assembly, embryonic development, translation and protein phosphorylation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p026.s002: To determine the drought-responsive modules comprised of functionally similar genes, we carried out gene ontology (GO) enrichment analyses for all modules. PMC3360736.p026.s003: Functional enrichment revealed that these drought-responsive module genes were preferentially involved in the biological processes of stimulus response, photosynthesis, nucleosome assembly, embryonic development, translation and protein amino acid phosphorylation (Figure 4; a full list is provided in Table S3).

events.event_label: Module participates in gene co-expression network and is associated with stimulus response

PMC3360736.p027.s003: There are a total of 119 genes whose functions are associated with responses to environmental stimuli, which represent approximately half of the annotated genes in the module. PMC3360736.p027.s004: Most genes in the module are downregulated by drought stress, while only 16 genes are induced by dehydration, which implies that the module may serve as a negative regulator in rice response to drought stress.

events.event_label: drought stress alters expression of modules associated with drought response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p041.s001: The primary goal of this analysis was to identify functional modules in the co-expression network that were significantly associated with drought-stress response in rice. PMC3360736.p041.s002: To identify such modules, we used the average value of the absolute log2-fold expression changes of module genes to measure module changes in the response to drought stress. PMC3360736.p041.s003: The significances of module changes greater than the network average were assessed using t-tests in the drought-tolerant (N22, Azucena and Bala) and drought-sensitive (IR64) rice cultivars based on the independent datasets. events.event_label: module expressed in root, leaf and shoot
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p033.s001: Expression patterns of drought-responsive module genes were investigated in six different tissue types, including the anther, embryo, root, leaf, shoot and shoot apical meristem (SAM), which are shown in Figure 5. events.event_label: multiple motifs associate with the module
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p037.s006: A comparison of the discovered motifs with the cis-regulatory elements from the PLACE and AGRIS databases showed that some motifs, such as ABA-responsive element (ABRE) and GCC-box, were known to be involved in drought stress response. PMC3360736.p037.s007: As expected, the ABRE element with the conserved GCCACGTGKC sequence was found to be enriched in six modules, including 4, 8, and 14, which were significantly induced by drought stress. PMC3360736.p037.s008: Among those revealed elements, most motifs were described to be involved in known functions, while only one motif with the AGCTAGCTAG sequence had an unknown function. PMC3360736.p037.s009: We found many of discovered motifs were enriched in more than one drought-responsive gene module, especially the three global motifs that included the REGION1OSOSEM (CSGCGGCGGC), the SBOXATRBCS (CCCCCTCC) and the GAGA8HVBKN3 (GGGAGGGAG) element which occurred in nine or more modules. PMC3360736.p037.s010: Interestingly, the RY/Sph box with the conserved CATGCATGCA sequence and the AGCTAGCTAG element were identified to be uniquely enriched in module 1. PMC3360736.p037.s011: Although the role of the two motifs in drought stress response remains unclear, the root-specific expression of associated genes suggests that both of them are likely to be involved in transcriptional repression in rice root in response to drought stress.

events.event_label: drought stress affects drought stress response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p004.s001: Drought is a major environmental stress factor that affects the growth and development of plants. PMC3360736.p004.s003: The increasing molecular information has shown complex gene networks that consist of numerous different genes are involved in plant responses to drought stress. evidence.text: We identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress; 1,392 genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 gene modules.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p002.s001: Understanding the molecular mechanisms that underlie plant responses to drought stress is challenging due to the complex interplay of numerous different genes. PMC3360736.p002.s002: Here, we used network-based gene clustering to uncover the relationships between drought-responsive genes from large microarray datasets. PMC3360736.p002.s003: We identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress; 1,392 genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 gene modules. PMC3360736.p002.s004: These drought-responsive gene modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and transcription factor binding sites.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Understanding the molecular mechanisms that underlie plant responses to drought stress is challenging due to the complex interplay of numerous different genes.
Here, we used network-based gene clustering to uncover the relationships between drought-responsive genes from large microarray datasets.
We identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress; 1,392 genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 gene modules.

These drought-responsive gene modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and transcription factor binding sites.

Among those revealed elements, most motifs were described to be involved in known functions, while only one motif with the AGCTAGCTAG sequence had an unknown function.

We found many of discovered motifs were enriched in more than one drought-responsive gene module, especially the three global motifs that included the REGION1OSOSEM (CSGCGGCGGC), the SBOXATRBCS (CCCCCTCC) and the GAGA8HVBKN3 (GGGAGGGAG) element which occurred in nine or more modules.

evidence_context_sentences.text: The primary goal of this analysis was to identify functional modules in the co-expression network that were significantly associated with drought-stress response in rice.
To identify such modules, we used the average value of the absolute log2-fold expression changes of module genes to measure module changes in the response to drought stress.

The significances of module changes greater than the network average were assessed using t-tests in the drought-tolerant (N22, Azucena and Bala) and drought-sensitive (IR64) rice cultivars based on the independent datasets.
The data showed there were one to five modules whose expression changes were significantly greater than the network average in each rice variety (Table 1).

relations.subject: drought stress

evidence.text: From publically available microarray data, we identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress and more than half of the drought-responsive genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 functional modules.

relations.evidence_preview: From publically available microarray data, we identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress and more than half of the drought-responsive genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 functional modules.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC3360736.p058.s001: The major objective for this study was to use the network-based approach to identify drought-responsive gene modules, providing new insights into the organization of functional modules in the response to drought stress. PMC3360736.p058.s002: From publically available microarray data, we identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress and more than half of the drought-responsive genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 functional modules. PMC3360736.p058.s003: These correlated transcriptional modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and TF binding sites. PMC3360736.p058.s004: Functional annotations show that some modules are enriched for genes involved in the response to stress, including OsbZip23, OsbZip72 and AP37 which have been validated as important factors affecting drought tolerance traits for rice.

evidence_context_sentences.text: The major objective for this study was to use the network-based approach to identify drought-responsive gene modules, providing new insights into the organization of functional modules in the response to drought stress.

From publically available microarray data, we identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress and more than half of the drought-responsive genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 functional modules.

These correlated transcriptional modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and TF binding sites.

Functional annotations show that some modules are enriched for genes involved in the response to stress, including OsbZip23, OsbZip72 and AP37 which have been validated as important factors affecting drought tolerance traits for rice.

In cyanobacteria thylakoids are of various shapes and are attached to, or continuous with, the plasma membrane. sentences.text: Understanding the molecular mechanisms that underlie plant responses to drought stress is challenging due to the complex interplay of numerous different genes.
Here, we used network-based gene clustering to uncover the relationships between drought-responsive genes from large microarray datasets.
We identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress; 1,392 genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 gene modules.

These drought-responsive gene modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and transcription factor binding sites.
Drought is a major environmental stress factor that affects the growth and development of plants.
Plant drought stress response is one of the most complex biological processes, and it involves numerous changes at the physiological, cellular, and molecular levels.
The increasing molecular information has shown complex gene networks that consist of numerous different genes are involved in plant responses to drought stress.
Many genes with various functions have been identified to be involved in the response of plants to drought stress, but little is known about the relationships between these genes.
To determine whether these gene modules in our drought-responsive gene network were robust, another gene correlation network was generated using the WGCNA package.
By using this method, a total of 16 modules of highly correlated genes were detected in the WGCNA correlation network.
Although the number of gene modules of two networks is not equal, module assignment of our network was highly preserved in the WGCNA network (Figure 4).
These data indicated that our method was robust for the construction of gene correlation networks and module detection.
To determine the drought-responsive modules comprised of functionally similar genes, we carried out gene ontology (GO) enrichment analyses for all modules.

Functional enrichment revealed that these drought-responsive module genes were preferentially involved in the biological processes of stimulus response, photosynthesis, nucleosome assembly, embryonic development, translation and protein amino acid phosphorylation (Figure 4; a full list is provided in Table S3).
Module 1 consists of 303 gene nodes and is the largest module in the drought-responsive gene co-expression network.
It is not surprising that the module is enriched with stimulus response genes (GO: 0050896, FDR = 2.
There are a total of 119 genes whose functions are associated with responses to environmental stimuli, which represent approximately half of the annotated genes in the module.
Another important negative player is module 2, which contains 213 drought-responsive genes; 189 genes are downregulated by drought stress.
Functional enrichment shows that the module genes are enriched with products that target the thylakoid (GO: 0009579, FDR = 1.
Module 4 is enriched for genes known to control the response of plants to water (GO: 0009415, FDR = 3.
The module contains five genes involved in this process, which accounted for 41.
6% of the annotated water responsive genes in the background datasets.
Module 8 is another module that is upregulated by drought stress, which is principally involved in the response to abiotic stimuli.
The gene module contains 14 abiotic stimulus responsive genes (GO: 0009628, FDR = 1.
9×10−5), representing approximately 38% of all of the 37 annotated genes in the module.
Modules 6 and 10 are other two important gene groups that are preferentially involved in the processes of protein amino acid phosphorylation (GO: 0006468, FDR = 7.
1×10−12 for module 6, and FDR = 3.
8×10−3 for module 10, respectively).

Expression patterns of drought-responsive module genes were investigated in six different tissue types, including the anther, embryo, root, leaf, shoot and shoot apical meristem (SAM), which are shown in Figure 5.
As the largest gene group in the drought-responsive network, module 1 is enriched for stimulus response genes with high expression levels in roots.
Transcripts of module 2 are abundant in leaves and shoots, which is consistent with the fact that the module genes are preferentially involved in photosynthesis.
Not surprisingly, module 4, which is enriched for LEA genes, is highly expressed in the embryo.
Tissue-specific expression of genes in the drought-responsive modules.
A total of 51 putative cis-motifs were found in 15 drought-responsive modules with MEME E-value less than 1.

The Pearson correlation coefficient between each motif pair was calculated based on the corresponding position weight matrices and similar motifs were clustered using a correlation coefficient cutoff of 0.
After merging motifs within each cluster, 10 nonredundant motifs were identified to be involved in the response to drought stress (Figure 6).

A comparison of the discovered motifs with the cis-regulatory elements from the PLACE and AGRIS databases showed that some motifs, such as ABA-responsive element (ABRE) and GCC-box, were known to be involved in drought stress response.
As expected, the ABRE element with the conserved GCCACGTGKC sequence was found to be enriched in six modules, including 4, 8, and 14, which were significantly induced by drought stress.
Among those revealed elements, most motifs were described to be involved in known functions, while only one motif with the AGCTAGCTAG sequence had an unknown function.

We found many of discovered motifs were enriched in more than one drought-responsive gene module, especially the three global motifs that included the REGION1OSOSEM (CSGCGGCGGC), the SBOXATRBCS (CCCCCTCC) and the GAGA8HVBKN3 (GGGAGGGAG) element which occurred in nine or more modules.
Interestingly, the RY/Sph box with the conserved CATGCATGCA sequence and the AGCTAGCTAG element were identified to be uniquely enriched in module 1.
The primary goal of this analysis was to identify functional modules in the co-expression network that were significantly associated with drought-stress response in rice.
To identify such modules, we used the average value of the absolute log2-fold expression changes of module genes to measure module changes in the response to drought stress.

The significances of module changes greater than the network average were assessed using t-tests in the drought-tolerant (N22, Azucena and Bala) and drought-sensitive (IR64) rice cultivars based on the independent datasets.
The data showed there were one to five modules whose expression changes were significantly greater than the network average in each rice variety (Table 1).
Module 4 consists of 134 genes with an average correlation coefficient of 0.
88, and most are induced by drought stress in different rice varieties.
Module 4 contains four genes encoding transcription factors from three families.
Interestingly, a heat shock transcription factor (HSF, LOC_Os01g39020.
1) induced by drought stress had the highest intramodular connectivity value in the drought-responsive TFs.
The HSF gene contained 35 co-expression neighbors and their average correlation to the locus was 0.
90 (Figure 7A).
Many HSF co-expression genes encode stress-related functional proteins, such as the LEA and dehydrin proteins.

Prediction of cis-regulatory elements revealed that the ABRE element and the GCC-box were conserved in these putative target genes; in particular, the ABRE element was found in all 35 neighboring genes.
Organization of hub genes on rice chromosomes
The highly connected hub genes within the networks are thought to play important roles in organizing the functional modules.
Here, we identified hub genes as the top 20% highly connected nodes in each module, and 262 hub genes were found in fifteen drought-responsive modules.

Functional enrichment revealed that these drought-responsive hub genes were preferentially involved in the response to stimulus (Table 2), which was in accordance with the important roles of these genes in response to drought stress.

The major objective for this study was to use the network-based approach to identify drought-responsive gene modules, providing new insights into the organization of functional modules in the response to drought stress.

From publically available microarray data, we identified 2,607 rice genes that showed significant changes in gene expression under drought stress and more than half of the drought-responsive genes were highly intercorrelated to form 15 functional modules.

These correlated transcriptional modules are biologically plausible, with enrichments for genes in common functional categories, stress response changes, tissue-specific expression and TF binding sites.

Functional annotations show that some modules are enriched for genes involved in the response to stress, including OsbZip23, OsbZip72 and AP37 which have been validated as important factors affecting drought tolerance traits for rice.

Although approximately 20% of module genes still have not been functionally annotated, the high degree of transcriptional connectivity allows us to infer the role of these novel genes in drought response based on the known annotations of the stress-related genes in the modules.
Among these identified drought-responsive modules, module 4 consisting of 134 genes was significantly associated with drought response in both drought-tolerant and drought-sensitive rice varieties.

The involvement of many of these genes in drought response was not previously known, but it was understood that they were involved in controlling the response of the plant to water and embryonic development.
Motif enrichment analysis revealed approximately 70% genes in the module contained the ABRE element in their promoter regions, and most of them are induced by drought stress.
The enrichment of ABRE element in module 4 suggests that the module may be involved in ABA-mediated drought response pathway.

The ABRE element has been shown to be recognized by the bZIP belonging to the AREB/ABFs family, but interestingly, but interestingly, we found a novel candidate gene in module 4 that encodes a heat shock transcription factor which binds to ABRE elements.

The HSF TF contains 35 putative target genes with at least one ABRE element in their promoter regions, implying that it is likely to serve as a key regulator of the module involved in drought response.
Gene organization in rice chromosomes is non-random with respect to the complex traits of drought tolerance.
The distribution of drought-responsive hub genes that was detected varied both among the rice chromosomes and within regions of each chromosome.
Many hub genes in the drought-responsive modules were clustered in rice chromosomes.
These hub gene clusters overlapped with QTLs for drought tolerance, which suggests that hub gene clusters are likely to be major components of drought tolerance QTLs.
